Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'm 29, working part-time, and juggling a lot of responsibilities with limited resources, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then I'd binge through materials. It wasn't organized at all—more like controlled chaos that somehow worked out in the end.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram everything in a few days before deadlines. It really depended on what else was going on in my life—work shifts, bills to sort out, just general life stuff that takes priority when you're older and don't have much of a safety net.
Q3: Usually it was because I knew an assignment was coming up or I'd gotten a notification about something due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important. It wasn't excitement or curiosity driving me—more like obligation and the fear of falling too far behind.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural habit for me. It was more like a chore I had to remind myself to do. I'd set reminders on my phone, and eventually checking the LMS became part of my weekly survival routine, but it never felt automatic or enjoyable.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them at my own pace, pause, rewind when I zoned out. The discussion boards were okay too—sometimes reading what other students wrote helped me understand things I'd missed. But honestly, I often felt too bored to engage deeply with anything.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. With limited time and low motivation, I had to prioritize what would directly affect my grades. If something wasn't going to be assessed, I just couldn't bring myself to care about it. It felt like a waste of energy I didn't have.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection between the activity and my grade, it felt worth doing. Also, if it was something visual or interactive rather than just walls of text. Anything that felt like busywork or padding made me tune out immediately. I needed to see the point.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely ignorable to me. I know that sounds bad, but when you're low on motivation and resources, you have to pick your battles. Required activities had stakes attached, so I did them. Optional ones just felt like extra weight I couldn't carry.
Q9: Yes, a lot actually. I'd download videos and PDFs and study them offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd take handwritten notes and review those instead of going back to the LMS. I also used some free YouTube videos to supplement topics I found confusing.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing my notes or rewatching downloaded lectures without being logged in anywhere. Sometimes I'd be working through problems on paper for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. My actual study time was probably higher than what any system could track.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with readings—I'd save them as PDFs and read them on my phone during breaks at work. The LMS only captured that initial download.
Q12: There were stretches where I just felt completely bored and disconnected. Nothing about the material excited me, and life stress made it worse. When money was tight or work was demanding, the course felt like this distant obligation I resented rather than something I was invested in.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a big one—after long work shifts, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled my attention away. Sometimes I'd open materials but be too mentally exhausted to actually engage with them properly.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through readings without really processing them. I was technically 'studying' but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and peer interactions a lot because they felt draining. I knew they mattered for participation, but interacting with others when you're feeling disengaged and bored is exhausting. I'd wait until the last minute and then force myself through it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS, cramming everything in. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback didn't really motivate me more—I'd just think 'okay, I passed' and move on. Critical feedback sometimes made me more anxious about the next assignment, but it didn't fundamentally change how I approached the course.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it didn't really pull me in. Having five courses at once meant I was spread thin. The course felt like it was designed for someone with more time and energy than I had. I just followed the minimum path to get through it.
Q19: The visual materials made things easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks or when expectations weren't clear. I needed straightforward, no-nonsense instructions because I didn't have the mental bandwidth to figure out ambiguous requirements.
Q20: I think that happens when people are anxious or confused. They might be clicking around trying to understand what's expected, looking for answers, but not actually producing work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that prevents them from following through despite their intentions.
Q21: That actually sounds like me. My attendance and login frequency weren't great, but I still managed decent grades because I studied effectively offline. I focused on what mattered for assessments and cut out everything else. The LMS activity doesn't capture that efficiency.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually thinking or just staring blankly. They miss offline studying, they miss the quality of engagement, and they definitely miss the emotional state behind the numbers. A bored student clicking through is very different from an engaged one.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not just completing tasks—it's being mentally present and curious. By that definition, I wasn't very engaged this course. I did the work, but my heart wasn't in it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. It wouldn't show that I was going through a difficult period financially and emotionally. It wouldn't capture that I completed everything despite having almost no motivation, which honestly took more effort than it would for someone who actually cared about the material.
